m_Item1.QuestionText: What is a partial class in .NET?

Options.OptionText: A class that can be split across multiple files
A class that can only contain partial methods
A class that can only be partially instantiated
A class that can only be used as a base class for other classes

m_Item1.Explanation: A partial class in .NET is a class that can be split across multiple files, which can be useful for organizing large code files or separating generated code from hand-written code. Partial classes are defined using the "partial" keyword, and each partial definition must have the same name and be defined in the same namespace.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is an abstract class in .NET?

Options.OptionText: A class that cannot be instantiated
A class that can only contain abstract methods
A class that can only be used as a base class for other classes
A class that can only contain static members

m_Item1.Explanation: An abstract class in .NET is a class that cannot be instantiated directly, but can be used as a base class for other classes. Abstract classes can contain abstract methods, which are methods that have no implementation and must be overridden by derived classes. Abstract classes can also contain non-abstract methods, properties, and fields, which can be used by derived classes. Abstract classes are typically used to define a common interface or behavior for a family of related classes.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following statements about a static class in C# is true?

Options.OptionText: It can be used as a base class for other classes.
It cannot contain static members.
It cannot be used as a namespace.
It cannot contain instance constructors.

m_Item1.Explanation: A static class in C# cannot be instantiated, and thus it cannot contain instance constructors (new keyword cannot be used with it). Static classes can only contain static members such as fields, properties, and methods.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is a partial class in C# primarily used for?

Options.OptionText: To allow a class to be marked as sealed.
To prevent other classes from inheriting from it.
To divide the definition of a class across multiple files.
To provide a way to define global variables.

m_Item1.Explanation: Partial classes in C# are used to split the definition of a class across multiple source files. This allows developers to work on different parts of the class independently, improving maintainability and organization of large classes or codebases.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following statements about an abstract class in C# is correct?

Options.OptionText: An abstract class can be instantiated using the new keyword.
An abstract class must contain only abstract methods.
An abstract class cannot be used as a parameter type in methods.
An abstract class may contain non-abstract (concrete) methods.

m_Item1.Explanation: An abstract class in C# is a class marked with the abstract keyword, and it can contain both abstract methods (which have no implementation and must be overridden in derived classes) and non-abstract methods (which have implementations and are inherited by derived classes).

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is a static class in .NET?

Options.OptionText: A class that can be inherited
A class that can only contain static members
A class that can be instantiated with the new keyword
A class that can only be used as a base class

m_Item1.Explanation: A static class in .NET is a class that can only contain static members, such as static methods, static fields, and static properties. It cannot be instantiated using the new keyword, and it cannot be used as a base class for other classes. Static classes are typically used to group related methods and properties that do not require instance-level state.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What could be the output of the following set of code?
using System;
class Program {
  static void Main(string[] args) {
    Console.WriteLine(vol(10));
    Console.WriteLine(vol(2.5 f, 5));
    Console.WriteLine(vol(5, 4, 5));
    Console.ReadLine();
  }
  static int vol(int x) {
    return (x * x * x);
  }
  static float vol(float r, int h) {
    return (3.14 f * r * r * h);
  }
  static long vol(long l, int b, int h) {
    return (l * b * h);
  }
}

Options.OptionText: compile time error

m_Item1.Explanation: This is example of polymorphism. In this Example vol method is overloaded with diffferent parameters. So First call will go to vol with int parameter , second will go to Vol with float and int parameter and last call will go to vol with long ,int and int parameter.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the given modifiers can be used to prevent Method overriding?

Options.OptionText: Static
Constant
Sealed
Final

m_Item1.Explanation: In C#, the sealed keyword is used to prevent further overriding of a method in derived classes. When you declare a method as sealed in a derived class, it means that the method cannot be overridden in any further derived classes.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
using System;
class Test {
  static void Main() {
    A.F();
    B.F();
  }
}
class A {
  static A() {
    Console.WriteLine("Init A");
  }
  public static void F() {
    Console.WriteLine("A.F");
  }
}
class B {
  static B() {
    Console.WriteLine("Init B");
  }
  public static void F() {
    Console.WriteLine("B.F");
  }
}
What will be the output

Options.OptionText:
Init A
A.F
Init B
B.F
None

Init B
B.F
Init A
A.F
Error

m_Item1.Explanation:
Initialization Sequence:
static A() and static B() are static constructors for classes A and B respectively. Static constructors are called automatically before the first instance is created or any static members are referenced.

Output Explanation:
A.F() is called first in Main(), which directly calls A.F, resulting in the output: A.F.
B.F() is called next in Main(), which directly calls B.F, resulting in the output: B.F.

Initialization Messages:
When the program starts, static A() prints Init A because A's static constructor executes before any static method or instance is created.

Similarly, static B() prints Init B for class B.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
class Program
{
    const int A = 5;
    static void Main()
    {
        Program c = new Program();
        Console.WriteLine(Program.A);
    }
}

Options.OptionText: Compile ti
none
run time error

m_Item1.Explanation:
In C#, const is a keyword used to declare constants. Constants are variables whose values cannot be changed once they are initialized.
Initialization: Constants must be initialized at the time of declaration, and their value cannot be modified later.
Type: Constants can be of any built-in C# type (int, float, double, char, etc.), including user-defined types if they are marked as const.
Scope: Constants are implicitly static, meaning they belong to the type, not to instances of the type. They are accessed using the type name.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the primary purpose of using a sealed class in C#?

Options.OptionText: To allow the class to be inherited by other classes
To prevent the class from being instantiated
To prevent the class from being used as a base class
To encapsulate data within the class

m_Item1.Explanation: A sealed class in C# is used to prevent other classes from inheriting from it, thereby stopping the inheritance chain. This is useful when a class's design needs to be fixed and should not be extended.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following statements about sealed classes in C# is true?

Options.OptionText: Sealed classes can only have static members.
Sealed classes cannot be instantiated.
Sealed classes can inherit from other classes.
Sealed classes must implement all inherited abstract members.

m_Item1.Explanation: Sealed classes in C# can inherit from other classes, but they cannot be used as base classes themselves. They can't be inherited by other classes.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In which scenario would you consider using a sealed class in your design?

Options.OptionText: When you want to make the class abstract.
When you want to ensure a class cannot be modified by changing its behavior through inheritance.
When you want the class to implement multiple interfaces.
When you want to ensure a class can only be instantiated once.

m_Item1.Explanation: A sealed class is used when you want to prevent further inheritance and modification of the class's behavior through inheritance, thereby ensuring the integrity of its implementation.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the result of attempting to inherit from a sealed class in C#?

Options.OptionText: A warning is generated at compile-time, but the code will execute.
The derived class will be created only if it contains non-sealed members.
A compile-time error is generated, preventing the code from compiling.
The derived class will override all methods from the sealed class.

m_Item1.Explanation: Attempting to inherit from a sealed class in C# results in a compile-time error, as sealed classes are specifically designed to prevent inheritance.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How does a sealed class impact performance in the context of method calls?

Options.OptionText: It allows the JIT compiler to perform optimizations, making method calls faster.
It makes method calls slower due to additional checks.
It has no impact on the performance of method calls.
It requires more memory allocation for each method call.

m_Item1.Explanation: Sealed classes can enhance performance because the JIT compiler can optimize method calls by knowing the exact type of the instance and eliminating the need for virtual method call lookups.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What does a 'sealed' method in C# achieve?

Options.OptionText: It prevents the method from being inherited by other classes.
It enhances the method's performance by optimizing it.
It allows the method to be inherited but not overridden.
It specifies that the method must be called on a sealed class.

m_Item1.Explanation: A sealed method in C# prevents the method from being further overridden by derived classes. This is useful when you want to limit the extensibility of your code.

m_Item1.QuestionText: When defining a sealed method in C#, where should the sealed keyword be placed?

Options.OptionText: Before the method's access modifier.
After the method's return type.
Before the override keyword.
After the method's parameter list.

m_Item1.Explanation: In C#, you place the sealed keyword before the override keyword. This indicates that the method is overriding a base class method and should not be further overridden in derived classes.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In which scenarios would you use a sealed method in C#?

Options.OptionText: When you want to enhance the method to be readonly.
When you want to prevent further modifications for performance reasons.
When you want to ensure no additional inheritance of the class.
When you want to restrict memory usage of the method.

m_Item1.Explanation: Sealed methods might be used to prevent further modifications in order to maintain performance or a specific behavior. It can prevent the method from being overridden in subclasses.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What must be true in order to declare a sealed method in C#?

Options.OptionText: The method must be abstract.
The method must already be a static method.
The method must be an override method inherited from a base class.
The method must return void.

m_Item1.Explanation: Only methods that are overridden from a base class can be declared sealed. The sealed keyword is used to prevent the method from being further overridden.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the purpose of using a sealed keyword with a method?

Options.OptionText: To stop the method from throwing exceptions.
To make the method execute faster.
To stop derived classes from overriding the method.
To allow only specific classes to access the method.

m_Item1.Explanation: The sealed keyword is used in method declaration to stop derived classes from overriding that method, thus maintaining a consistent behavior across frameworks.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is a primary characteristic of static classes in C#?

Options.OptionText: They can be instantiated.
They cannot be instantiated and must have all static members.
They can inherit from other classes.
They can have instance members.

m_Item1.Explanation: Static classes cannot be instantiated and must have all static members.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following statements about static classes is true?

Options.OptionText: Static classes can be sealed.
Static classes can implement interfaces.
Static classes can contain non-static members.
Static classes can have destructors.

m_Item1.Explanation: Static classes can implement interfaces because interfaces can contain static methods and properties.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Why would you use a static class in C#?

Options.OptionText: To enable inheritance and polymorphism.
To encapsulate utility methods or constants.
To allow creating multiple instances.
To bind runtime data to instances.

m_Item1.Explanation: Static classes are often used to encapsulate utility methods or constants, benefiting from their ability to be accessed without needing an instance of the class.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How are members of a static class accessed in C#?

Options.OptionText: Using an instance of the class.
Directly through the class name.
Via the 'new' keyword.
Using a pointer to the class.

m_Item1.Explanation: Members of a static class are accessed directly through the class name as static classes cannot be instantiated.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is a valid declaration of a static class in C#?

Options.OptionText: static class Utils { }
public static new class Utils { }
class static Utils { }
static sealed class Utils { }

m_Item1.Explanation: The correct syntax for declaring a static class in C# is 'static class ClassName'. It cannot have other modifiers like new or sealed in its declaration.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the primary characteristic of a static method in C#?

Options.OptionText: It can access instance variables directly.
It is invoked on the instance of the class.
It belongs to the instance of the class.
It belongs to the class itself rather than any object instance.

m_Item1.Explanation: Static methods are associated with the class itself, not with any particular instance of the class. Hence, they can be called without creating an instance of the class.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How do you call a static method named 'Compute' from a class named 'Calculator' in C#?

Options.OptionText: new Calculator().Compute();
Calculator.Compute();
Compute();
CalculatorInstance.Compute();

m_Item1.Explanation: Static methods are called using the class name followed by the method name. They do not require an object instance to be invoked.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following describes a limitation of static methods in C#?

Options.OptionText: Static methods can only access other static members within the class.
Static methods can only be declared in static classes.
Static methods cannot be overloaded.
Static methods can exist without any variables.

m_Item1.Explanation: Static methods can only access static members (variables and other methods) of the class because they do not have access to any specific instance of the class.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which keyword is used to define a static method in C#?

Options.OptionText: this
readonly
static
private

m_Item1.Explanation: The 'static' keyword is used to declare a static method, indicating that the method belongs to the class itself rather than an instance of the class.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What happens if a static method accesses an instance variable directly in C#?

Options.OptionText: The method will compile and run without errors.
A compile-time error occurs.
The method will execute but with incorrect values.
The variable is automatically converted to static.

m_Item1.Explanation: A static method cannot access instance variables directly since there is no instance context. Attempting to do so results in a compile-time error.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following correctly describes the behavior of a static field in a C# class?

Options.OptionText: It is shared across all instances of a class.
Each instance of the class has its own copy.
It can only be accessed from outside the class.
It cannot be modified once set.

m_Item1.Explanation: Static fields are shared among all instances of a class, meaning that each instance accesses the same memory location for that field.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How can you access a static method in a C# class?

Options.OptionText: By creating an instance of the class.
By using the 'this' keyword.
By using the class name directly.
By using the 'new' keyword.

m_Item1.Explanation: Static methods are called using the class name since they belong to the class itself rather than any specific instance.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What will happen if you try to access a non-static member from within a static method in a C# class?

Options.OptionText: It will compile and execute successfully.
It will throw a runtime exception.
It will result in a compile-time error.
It will automatically convert the non-static member to static.

m_Item1.Explanation: Static methods cannot access instance-level members (non-static members) directly because they do not belong to a particular instance.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is true about static constructors in C#?

Options.OptionText: A class can have multiple static constructors.
Static constructors can take parameters.
Static constructors are called automatically before any static members are accessed.
Static constructors are called only once per instance.

m_Item1.Explanation: A static constructor is called automatically before any static members are accessed, and it runs only once when the class is loaded.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What keyword should be used to declare a static class in C#?

Options.OptionText: abstract
static
const
readonly

m_Item1.Explanation: The keyword 'static' is used to declare a static class, which means that it cannot be instantiated and all its members must be static.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is a key benefit of using partial classes in C#?

Options.OptionText: They automatically reduce the size of the compiled assembly.
They allow a class to be split into multiple files, improving organization.
They enable multiple inheritance in C#.
They provide a way to add methods dynamically at runtime.

m_Item1.Explanation: Partial classes allow a class to be split across multiple files, which can help organize large classes and make collaborative development easier.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How are partial class files related within a project?

Options.OptionText: They must be in the same directory.
They must all include a reference to each other using 'using' directives.
They simply need the same class name and 'partial' keyword, regardless of location.
They must explicitly inherit from each other.

m_Item1.Explanation: Partial class files need to use the 'partial' keyword and have the same class name, even if they are in different directories.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is true about partial classes?

Options.OptionText: All partial files must be compiled together.
They enable real-time class updating during debugging.
They can each have different access modifiers within the same class.
They eliminate the need for namespaces.

m_Item1.Explanation: All parts of a partial class must be available and compiled together to create a valid class definition.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is required for a class to be correctly split across multiple files as a partial class?

Options.OptionText: Each file must have a unique namespace.
Each file must define a unique constructor.
Each file must declare the class using the 'partial' keyword.
Each file must include 'static' keyword if the class is static.

m_Item1.Explanation: To split a class across multiple files using partial classes, the 'partial' keyword must be used in each file where the class is declared.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following can NOT be achieved using partial classes?

Options.OptionText: Enhancing code readability by organizing code logically.
Dividing a large class into manageable parts for different developers to work on simultaneously.
Changing access levels of variables defined within different partial class files.
Extending a class's functionality without modifying the original code file directly.

m_Item1.Explanation: Partial classes cannot change access levels of variables; all parts of a partial class must adhere to the same access level rules as a non-partial class.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is a key characteristic of a partial method in C#?

Options.OptionText: It must have a return type.
It must have an implementation in a partial class.
It can have multiple definitions in non-partial classes.
It allows optional implementation.

m_Item1.Explanation: Partial methods allow a method declaration to be defined in one part of a partial class, and optionally implemented in another part. If the implementation is omitted, the compiler will not include calls to it, treating it as if it doesn't exist.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following statements is TRUE about parameters in partial methods?

Options.OptionText: Partial methods can have ref or out parameters.
Partial methods cannot include parameters.
Partial methods must have at least one parameter.
Partial methods can only have optional parameters.

m_Item1.Explanation: Partial methods can include parameters, including 'ref' or 'out' modifiers, allowing them to support the same parameter types as non-partial methods.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is NOT allowed for a partial method in C#?

Options.OptionText: Declaring a private access modifier explicitly
Including an out parameter
Defining it without an implementation
Having a return type other than void

m_Item1.Explanation: Partial methods in C# are not allowed to have return types. They can only be defined using the 'void' return type and cannot return a value.

m_Item1.QuestionText: When is the implementation of a partial method required in C#?

Options.OptionText: When it is declared with public access modifier
It is never required
When it has an out parameter
When it is used in multiple classes

m_Item1.Explanation: The implementation of a partial method is never required. If not implemented, any calls to the method are removed during compilation, making them effectively non-existent.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What happens if a partial method is declared but not implemented?

Options.OptionText: It causes a compilation error.
It leads to a runtime exception.
The declaration is ignored, and calls are removed.
It results in a warning at compile time.

m_Item1.Explanation: If a partial method is declared but not implemented, the compiler ignores the declaration, and any potential calls to the method are removed from the compiled code, resulting in no effect.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is true about abstract classes in C#?

Options.OptionText: Abstract classes cannot have constructors.
Abstract classes can have static methods.
Abstract classes cannot be inherited.
Abstract classes must contain at least one abstract method.

m_Item1.Explanation: Abstract classes can have static methods, concrete methods, and constructors. They do not necessarily need to have abstract methods.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Can an instance of an abstract class be created in C#?

Options.OptionText: Yes, using the 'new' keyword.
Yes, using a derived class.
No, abstract classes cannot be instantiated directly.
Yes, using reflection.

m_Item1.Explanation: You cannot create an instance of an abstract class directly. They must be inherited and instantiated through a derived class.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What access modifier is typically used for abstract methods in C#?

Options.OptionText: Protected
Public
Private
Internal

m_Item1.Explanation: Abstract methods are typically marked as protected so they can be implemented by derived classes.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of these statements is correct regarding abstract classes and interfaces in C#?

Options.OptionText: Interfaces can have constructors.
Abstract classes cannot have fields.
Abstract classes can implement methods.
Interfaces can contain fields.

m_Item1.Explanation: Abstract classes can provide implementation for some methods, unlike interfaces which cannot implement any methods.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In C#, what keyword is used to declare an abstract class?

Options.OptionText: base
class
interface
abstract

m_Item1.Explanation: The 'abstract' keyword is used to declare an abstract class in C#.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What will happen if an abstract class contains a constructor?

Options.OptionText: It will cause a compile-time error.
It can initialize some fields upon object creation of derived class.
The constructor will be ignored.
Abstract classes cannot have constructors.

m_Item1.Explanation: An abstract class can have a constructor, which can be used to initialize fields when a derived class is instantiated.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following can abstract classes not contain?

Options.OptionText: Static variables
Methods with implementations
Abstract methods
Instances of the abstract class

m_Item1.Explanation: Abstract classes cannot be instantiated directly, thus cannot contain instances of themselves.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which declaration choice is appropriate for ensuring that a derived class must implement certain methods?

Options.OptionText: Protected virtual
Private static
Abstract
Final

m_Item1.Explanation: Methods without implementation in an abstract class are declared as abstract. Derived classes must implement these methods.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is a characteristic of an abstract method in C#?

Options.OptionText: It can have a body.
It must be declared in a non-abstract class.
It cannot be overridden in derived classes.
It must be implemented in a derived class.

m_Item1.Explanation: An abstract method in C# does not have a body and must be implemented in a derived class.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Where can abstract methods be declared in C#?

Options.OptionText: In a static class.
In a sealed class.
In an abstract class only.
In any class type.

m_Item1.Explanation: Abstract methods can only be declared within abstract classes in C#.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which keyword is used to indicate that a method in a class is abstract in C#?

Options.OptionText: virtual
override
new
abstract

m_Item1.Explanation: The 'abstract' keyword is used in C# to declare a method as abstract, meaning it does not have a body and must be implemented in derived classes.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What will happen if a derived class does not implement an abstract method from its base class?

Options.OptionText: The program will run successfully without any error.
The derived class will also become abstract.
The method in the derived class will automatically be a virtual method.
A runtime exception will occur when the program runs.

m_Item1.Explanation: If a derived class does not implement an abstract method from its base class, the derived class must also be declared abstract.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Can an abstract class in C# have non-abstract methods and fields?

Options.OptionText: Yes, but fields must be static.
No, it can only contain abstract methods.
Yes, it can contain both abstract and non-abstract methods and fields.
Only if methods are virtual.

m_Item1.Explanation: An abstract class in C# can contain abstract methods, non-abstract methods, and fields of any kind.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Is it possible to create an instance of an abstract class in C#?

Options.OptionText: Yes, using the 'new' keyword.
Yes, but only within derived classes.
No, abstract classes cannot be instantiated.
Yes, with permission from derived classes.

m_Item1.Explanation: Abstract classes in C# cannot be instantiated directly. They are intended to be base classes for other classes.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How can abstract methods in C# affect polymorphism?

Options.OptionText: They cannot be used in polymorphism.
They allow dynamic method resolution at runtime.
They enforce method overriding with no room for polymorphism.
Abstract methods are only used for static method dispatch.

m_Item1.Explanation: Abstract methods are crucial in polymorphism as they allow derived classes to provide implementations for methods declared in base classes, enabling dynamic method resolution at runtime.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following statements is true about abstract classes and methods in C#?

Options.OptionText: An abstract class can be instantiated directly.
Abstract methods must be static.
A class inheriting an abstract class must implement all abstract methods.
Abstract method declarations include the 'override' keyword.

m_Item1.Explanation: A class inheriting an abstract class must implement all of the abstract methods from the parent class unless the subclass is also abstract.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is operator overloading in C#?

Options.OptionText: The ability to assign multiple values to a single variable.
The ability to use an operator in both unary and binary operations.
The ability to redefine built-in operators for user-defined types.
The ability to override methods from the base class.

m_Item1.Explanation: Operator overloading in C# allows developers to redefine the operators so they can work with user-defined data types. It enhances code readability when custom operations need to be applied to objects.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which keyword is used to declare an operator overload method in C#?

Options.OptionText: operator
overload
override
overload_operator

m_Item1.Explanation: The 'operator' keyword is used in C# to declare an operator overload method, allowing you to define custom behavior for operators applied to class or struct instances.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In C#, which of the following operators cannot be overloaded?
m_Item1.Explanation: The assignment operator '=' cannot be overloaded in C#. While many operators can be redefined for custom types, the assignment operator is reserved with specific semantics by the language.

m_Item1.QuestionText: If a class overloads the '==' operator, which other operator must it also overload to maintain consistency?
m_Item1.Explanation: When overloading the '==' operator, it is required to also overload the '!=' operator to ensure logical consistency of equality and inequality comparisons.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is true about overloading binary operators in C#?

Options.OptionText: The operator method must have exactly one parameter.
The operator method must have exactly two parameters.
Binary operators cannot be overloaded.
Binary operators must return a void type.

m_Item1.Explanation: When overloading binary operators in C#, the operator method must have exactly two parameters, as it involves an operation between two operands.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the main purpose of using a sealed class in .NET?

Options.OptionText: To allow inheritance and method overriding.
To prevent a class from being inherited.
To allow multiple inheritance capabilities.
To ensure that each object has a unique identity.

m_Item1.Explanation: A sealed class in .NET is used to prevent further inheritance, meaning no class can derive from a sealed class.

m_Item1.QuestionText: When should a method in a class be declared as sealed?

Options.OptionText: When you want the method to be overridable in derived classes.
When the method is static and non-virtual.
When you want to prevent further overriding in derived classes.
When you want to provide a default implementation for all derived classes.

m_Item1.Explanation: A sealed method is used to prevent a derived class from further overriding it, ensuring no further modifications can be made to the method behavior.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which keyword is used to declare a class as sealed in C#?

Options.OptionText: final
immutable
static
sealed

m_Item1.Explanation: The keyword 'sealed' is used in C# to declare a class as sealed, preventing other classes from inheriting it.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following statements is true about sealed methods?

Options.OptionText: They can be overridden in derived classes.
They must be declared in an abstract class.
They can only exist within a sealed class.
They are used to prevent further overriding of virtual methods in derived classes.

m_Item1.Explanation: Sealed methods are used in a derived class to prevent further overriding of a virtual method, effectively sealing the method's current implementation.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Can a sealed class contain sealed methods in C#?

Options.OptionText: Yes, but only if they are private.
Yes, any method in a sealed class can be sealed.
No, sealed methods can only be in non-sealed classes.
No, sealed methods are not allowed in C#.

m_Item1.Explanation: Sealed methods can exist in non-sealed classes where they prevent overridden methods from further overrides, not needed in sealed classes as they cannot be inherited.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is a common scenarios for using sealed classes?

Options.OptionText: When designing a class library and you want to allow maximum flexibility for the end-users.
When a class serves as a specialized implementation that should not be deviated from.
When designing base classes intended for future extensibility.
When designing classes that are intended to be derived frequently.

m_Item1.Explanation: Sealed classes are often used to provide a specialized implementation that should not be altered through inheritance, ensuring consistency and preventing misuse.

m_Item1.QuestionText: If a method in a base class is marked as virtual, how can it be prevented from further overriding in a derived class?

Options.OptionText: By marking the method as abstract in the derived class.
By marking the method as static in the derived class.
By marking the method as sealed in the derived class.
By marking the method as const in the derived class.

m_Item1.Explanation: By using the 'sealed' keyword in the derived class, you prevent further overriding of the method, securing its current implementation.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following cannot be a feature of a sealed class in .NET?

Options.OptionText: It can contain static methods.
It can be instantiated.
It can be used as a base class.
It can contain instance methods.

m_Item1.Explanation: A sealed class cannot be used as a base class because the primary feature of a sealed class is that it cannot be extended or inherited.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What happens if a method is both sealed and static?

Options.OptionText: It provides hybrid functionality and can be overridden.
It is invalid since static methods cannot be sealed.
It becomes a constant method.
It automatically becomes virtual.

m_Item1.Explanation: Static methods cannot be sealed because they do not participate in inheritance hierarchy and overriding, making sealing them irrelevant.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Does a sealed class in C# have any impact on method performance?

Options.OptionText: No, there is no impact on method performance.
Yes, methods in a sealed class run faster due to lack of virtual table overhead.
Yes, methods in a sealed class run slower due to additional security checks.
Performance is variable and architecture dependent.

m_Item1.Explanation: Methods in a sealed class can be optimized by the compiler for performance because there is no need for virtual table (v-table) for dispatching calls, as they can't be overridden.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is a characteristic of a static class in .NET?

Options.OptionText: It can be instantiated.
It can inherit from another class.
It cannot contain non-static members.
It can implement interfaces.

m_Item1.Explanation: A static class cannot contain non-static members because it cannot be instantiated.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following statements about static methods in .NET is correct?

Options.OptionText: Static methods can access non-static fields directly.
Static methods can be overridden like instance methods.
Static methods belong to the class itself rather than any object instance.
You must instantiate a class to use its static methods.

m_Item1.Explanation: Static methods belong to the class itself and do not require an object instance to be called.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which access modifier is allowed for a static class in .NET?

Options.OptionText: protected
internal
private
public

m_Item1.Explanation: Static classes in .NET can be declared as public or internal, but not as protected or private.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What happens if you try to instantiate a static class?

Options.OptionText: A compilation error occurs.
An exception is thrown at runtime.
It behaves like a singleton instance.
The instantiation succeeds without errors.

m_Item1.Explanation: Attempting to instantiate a static class results in a compilation error because static classes cannot be instantiated.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Can a static method be overloaded in .NET?

Options.OptionText: Yes, static methods can be overloaded.
No, static methods cannot be overloaded.
Only instance methods can be overloaded.
Overloading is supported only for non-static methods.

m_Item1.Explanation: Static methods can be overloaded like instance methods by providing different parameter lists.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is true for a .NET static class?

Options.OptionText: It can have instance constructors.
It can use the 'new' keyword.
It can have only static constructors.
It can inherit from any other class type.

m_Item1.Explanation: A static class can only have static constructors because it cannot be instantiated.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following keywords can be applied to a static method?

Options.OptionText: abstract
virtual
override
none of the above

m_Item1.Explanation: Static methods cannot be abstract, virtual, or overridden.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In which scenario would you use a static method?

Options.OptionText: When a method requires access to instance data.
When a utility method requires handling common logic.
When needing to run different method implementations in derived classes.
When the method is unique to an object instance.

m_Item1.Explanation: Static methods are ideal for utility methods that handle common logic and do not require instance data.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What can a static constructor in a .NET class initialize?

Options.OptionText: Only instance fields
Only static fields
Both instance and static fields
Local variables

m_Item1.Explanation: A static constructor is used to initialize static fields of a class.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following statements is true about static fields in .NET?

Options.OptionText: Static fields are scoped to the instance of the class.
Each object of the class has its own copy of static fields.
Static fields are shared among all instances of a class.
Static fields cannot be initialized inside a static constructor.

m_Item1.Explanation: Static fields are shared across all instances of a class, meaning they are the same for every object of that class.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the primary purpose of using partial classes in .NET?

Options.OptionText: To increase code execution speed.
To allow a class to inherit from multiple classes.
To split the definition of a class into multiple files.
To provide a way to define private methods in a class.

m_Item1.Explanation: Partial classes in .NET are used to split the definition of a class into multiple files, which can be useful for organizing code and working in collaborative environments where multiple developers can work on different parts of the class.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How do partial methods differ from regular methods in .NET?

Options.OptionText: Partial methods must always return a value.
Partial methods must have parameters.
Partial methods can be left unimplemented without causing a compilation error.
Partial methods can be only accessed by public classes.

m_Item1.Explanation: Partial methods in .NET can be declared but not implemented, allowing them to be defined only in some parts of the partial class. If they are not implemented, the compiler ignores these declarations without causing any error.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following statements is true about partial classes in .NET?

Options.OptionText: Partial classes allow a class to belong to multiple namespaces.
Partial classes cannot include events or delegates.
Partial classes must be placed in the same namespace and assembly.
Partial classes must define all class members in each file.

m_Item1.Explanation: Partial class files must be in the same namespace and assembly in .NET. They act as one class at runtime, so their definitions need to be consistent and in a single compilation unit.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which keyword is used to define partial methods within a partial class?

Options.OptionText: virtual
partial
abstract
static

m_Item1.Explanation: In .NET, the 'partial' keyword is used to define partial methods within partial classes. This designates that a method may be implemented or omitted without affecting the class as a whole.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What happens if a partial method is declared but not implemented in any part of the partial class?

Options.OptionText: The compilation will fail with an error.
The method will be treated as an abstract method requiring implementation in a derived class.
The method will simply be ignored by the compiler.
The method will be automatically assigned a default implementation.

m_Item1.Explanation: If a partial method is declared but not implemented, it is ignored by the .NET compiler. This allows for designing optional methods that do not require implementation unless necessary.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Can a partial class be nested within another class in .NET?

Options.OptionText: Yes, a partial class can be nested.
No, partial classes must be top-level classes.
Yes, but only if the parent class is also partial.
No, nested classes cannot be partial in any way.

m_Item1.Explanation: In .NET, a partial class can indeed be nested within another class. This gives the developer flexibility in organizing code and defining complex class structures.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following exists exclusively when using partial methods in .NET?

Options.OptionText: They must always return a string.
They cannot have out parameters.
They must be public.
They can be defined outside the partial class block.

m_Item1.Explanation: Partial methods in .NET cannot have out parameters. This constraint is due to partial methods potentially being unimplemented, meaning out parameters would not be initialized.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is a significant limitation of partial classes in .NET?

Options.OptionText: Partial classes cannot be serialized.
Partial classes are not supported in Windows applications.
Partial classes cannot span across assemblies.
Partial classes require explicit instantiation.

m_Item1.Explanation: Partial classes must be contained within a single assembly. This ensures that all parts of the class are compiled together and can function as a single coherent unit.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In which scenario would partial classes be most beneficial?

Options.OptionText: When a class needs to be exported as a COM object.
When multiple developers are working on different features of the same class.
When a class needs extensive exception handling.
When implementing a class interface multiple times.

m_Item1.Explanation: Partial classes are particularly useful in collaborative development environments. They allow multiple developers to simultaneously work on different parts of the same class without file conflicts.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which visibility modifier do partial methods implicitly have in .NET?

Options.OptionText: Public
Internal
Private
Protected

m_Item1.Explanation: Partial methods in .NET are implicitly private, meaning they cannot be accessed outside of the class in which they are defined. This both limits their visibility and allows for optional implementation.
